summary: Jeff Atwood announces his $5,000 donation to the .NET open source community, fulfilling a promise made nearly a year earlier to give back advertising revenue. After months of deliberating over complex voting schemes, he simplifies the process and awards the entire grant to ScrewTurn Wiki based on community feedback. He emphasizes that open source projects are treated as second-class citizens in Microsoft's ecosystem and argues this must change. The post reflects on the personal financial risk of making this donation after leaving his job, while affirming his belief in the power of programmers building things together.
key_points: Open source projects are treated as second-class citizens in the Microsoft ecosystem, receiving virtually no support despite contributing significantly to the .NET community
Sometimes the best approach is to abandon elaborate plans and keep things simple - one winner gets everything rather than a complex voting bracket system
Giving back to the community is what makes the pain of advertising worthwhile and transforms a necessary evil into something meaningful
Real generosity involves some personal risk and discomfort - if it isn't a little scary, it's not worth doing
Microsoft matched the $5,000 contribution, bringing the total community investment to $10,000
money_quotes: Open source projects are treated as second-class citizens in the Microsoft ecosystem.
It's an open secret amongst bloggers that the blog comments are often better than the original blog post, and it's because the community collectively knows far more than you or I will ever know.
Giving back to the community is what makes the pain of advertising worthwhile.
But I figure if it isn't a little scary, it's not worth doing.
And I have found that you get back what you give, many times over.
There are no strings attached to this money of any kind. I trust the judgment of a fellow programmer to run their project as they see fit.
I wouldn't have kept banging out entries on this blog for the last four years if I didn't truly believe in the power of programmers collectively building useful stuff together.
key_insight: Supporting open source isn't just altruism—it's an investment in the programming community that returns value many times over, and meaningful giving should involve enough personal risk to feel a little scary.
